selection.greatFrontendReasonIfOmitted: I did not confirm a GreatFrontEnd page directly focused on the typeof operator, its null/array/NaN traps, or the undeclared-versus-TDZ nuance. Using an adjacent interview page would be weaker than direct typeof-focused references.

competitors.label: MDN typeof
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical reference for typeof semantics, return values, and special cases such as null and undeclared identifiers.
theirStrengths: It is the authoritative reference for typeof behavior and edge-case semantics.
theirGaps: The reference format is less compact as an interview answer and less grounded in one production normalization scenario.

realWorldUseCases: You receive mixed analytics events from multiple clients and must normalize payload fields before storing them.
MDN explains operator behavior accurately, but our page anchors the topic in a concrete analytics-payload normalization problem instead of staying reference-first.
actionableExamples: MDN still has strong operator examples and edge-case coverage, so the example criterion stays competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit about the undeclared-versus-TDZ distinction and about when typeof must be combined with Array.isArray and Number.isNaN.

actionableExamples: Add one compact Object.prototype.toString.call example if we need a clearer edge over the canonical docs later.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical typeof reference, but our page is stronger as an interview answer because it ties typeof to a real normalization workflow and calls out the TDZ and layered-type-check follow-ups more directly.

competitors.label: GeeksforGeeks typeof operator
competitors.selectionReason: This is a direct tutorial-style competitor for the typeof operator search intent.
theirStrengths: It is topic-direct and likely includes quick operator examples readers expect from the exact typeof query.
theirGaps: It is more introductory and less focused on production detection strategy.

realWorldUseCases: You receive mixed analytics events from multiple clients and must normalize payload fields before storing them.
followUpConfusion: Accessing them (even with typeof) inside TDZ throws ReferenceError.

realWorldUseCases: GeeksforGeeks is direct on the operator, but our page goes further into where typeof fails in production payload handling.
actionableExamples: A tutorial page for typeof still provides concrete examples, so the example criterion remains close.
followUpConfusion: Our page does more to separate plain-object checks from arrays, NaN, null, undeclared identifiers, and TDZ behavior.

actionableExamples: Keep the layered detection helper and the typeof-null code snippet close together so the page stays easy to reuse.

competitors.winReason: GeeksforGeeks covers the operator directly, but our page is more useful because it turns typeof into a layered type-detection strategy instead of only an operator definition with examples.

competitors.label: Why is typeof null "object"?
competitors.selectionReason: This is a focused community Q&A source for the single follow-up confusion that comes up most often in typeof interviews.
theirStrengths: It is tightly focused on the classic typeof-null confusion and usually gives historical context.
theirGaps: It is intentionally narrow and does not cover the broader runtime type-checking workflow.

realWorldUseCases: You receive mixed analytics events from multiple clients and must normalize payload fields before storing them.
followUpConfusion: Treating typeof value === 'object' as enough to detect plain objects.

realWorldUseCases: The Stack Overflow thread is useful for one quirk, but our page covers a broader production type-checking scenario.
actionableExamples: The thread can still be concrete on the null edge case, so the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: Our page now answers the bigger follow-up set in one place: typeof null, arrays, NaN, undeclared identifiers, and TDZ.

actionableExamples: If this page needs another pass later, add one shorter plain-object helper example for teams that rely on generic payload guards.

competitors.winReason: The Stack Overflow thread is strong on one quirk, but our page is better for this interview topic because it turns that quirk into a wider checklist for robust runtime type detection.
